sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 48-year-old male, presents with a history of a cough that has been present on and off for approximately three months.
He initially noticed it while loading horses and experienced an increase in coughing after a cold.
He has a history of exercise, including biking, and typically does not get sick.
He reports that his cough has resolved.
He has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly.
He is a former smoker, having quit approximately seven years ago.
He has a family history of breast cancer in his mother, but no history of lung cancer or other malignancies.
He denies any pain or shortness of breath.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly.
He is a former smoker, having quit approximately seven years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain or shortness of breath.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly for rheumatoid arthritis.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are within normal limits, with a blood pressure of 124/76, heart rate of 70, respiration rate of 16, and oxygen saturation of 98% on room air.
Physical exam: The patient's lungs are clear and equal bilateral, with no wheezing or crackles.
The heart has a regular rate and rhythm, with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs.
The extremities have no edema, but there is a slight swelling of the knuckles on both hands, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.
The skin is pink, warm, and dry, with brisk capillary refill.

Test Results: The patient had a chest CT scan that showed a solitary 2 cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which is smooth in appearance and shows no evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule that requires further evaluation and management.
Problem list: Lung nodule; Rheumatoid arthritis; History of smoking.
Plan:
I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for the patient's lung function.
I will also schedule a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
Based on the patient's smoking history and the characteristics of the nodule, I recommend removing it via video-assisted thoracoscopy.
The patient will be under general anesthesia for approximately 1.5 hours, and will have a chest tube until the following day.
They will be admitted to the hospital for one night and discharged the next day.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in a few minutes to complete the paperwork and begin the treatment process.
